title: X Archive Integrated
summary: The first public X history was cleaned, classified, and moved into the Laboratory Archive.
content: The early X feed contained everything: origin signals, noisy launch language, duplicate posts, CA drops, holder updates, failed plans, NFT discoveries, website work, and real machine incidents. The archive now keeps the useful signal and removes the disposable noise. Future Hosts can read the project as a build record instead of a feed full of hashtags.

title: Laboratory Console Activated
summary: Visitors can now communicate with the Laboratory through an in-universe command terminal.
content: The first interactive terminal entered production. The Laboratory now accepts commands, returns archive data, and responds like a system that is actively being used. This is not a developer console. It is an interface for Hosts who want to test the organism.

title: B20 Host Scanner Online
summary: The scanner became the primary interactive experiment inside the Laboratory.
content: The Host Scanner now simulates Base synchronization, parasite database loading, transaction fingerprint analysis, and randomized Host reports. No backend is required. The effect is fictional, but the interface is real enough to invite experimentation.

title: Laboratory Terminal Attached
summary: Passive system events began streaming into the Laboratory interface.
content: The Laboratory no longer waits for visitors to act. It prints fictional activity, updates traces, and keeps the operating system feeling alive even when no Host is touching the scanner.

title: Architecture Split
summary: The website moved from a single page prototype into a modular static system.
content: CSS, JavaScript, and data were split into focused modules. The root files remain stable entry points, while expandable behavior now lives under assets and data. Future updates should touch JSON first and HTML only when a new module shell is required.

title: Driver Booster Incident
summary: The Laboratory almost died because the only machine in the village stopped working.
content: Today's parasite was not B20. It was Driver Booster. One laptop, one village, one dead Windows installation, and the entire project paused with it. Five hours later the machine came back online. The Laboratory survived another day.

title: Windows Failure Detected
summary: Windows failed, but the Laboratory did not stop being alive.
content: A short emergency signal was posted when Windows died. The message was not polished because the situation was not polished. The important part was simple: the machine failed, the Laboratory did not.

title: Orbit Fuel Incident
summary: A real-world signal was archived as fictional laboratory evidence.
content: Cosmic fuel was delivered to orbit. No B20 parasites were detected in the real world yet. The post marked the point where outside events started being absorbed into the Laboratory language instead of treated as ordinary social content.

title: Still Alive
summary: The project survived the night and returned to building after real-world interruptions.
content: Website survived the night. Price survived the night. The founder survived the night. A few real-world side quests interrupted the build, but new ideas were still cooking and the Laboratory remained operational.

title: Day Two Complete
summary: The website survived launch, 22 Specimens were documented, and the experiment remained active.
content: Day two closed with 111 Hosts, a surviving website, and 22 documented Specimens. It was not a polished operation, but it was still moving. For a meme born from one post, survival itself became useful data.

title: Red Event Mutation
summary: The Genesis Colony mutated and the Red Event became part of the Specimen archive.
content: The colony mutated. Eleven new Specimens were documented, bringing the archive to 22. The Red Event introduced a second visual branch and confirmed that the NFT collection would behave like a living catalog, not a static product shelf.

title: Second Website Launched
summary: The second website went live after the first one failed almost immediately.
content: The founder launched a second website. The first one had been compromised in roughly 15 minutes. The new site was not perfect, but it existed, and existence mattered. The Laboratory had a public surface again.

title: Experiment #001 Opened
summary: The project reached 100 Hosts and opened its first public participation experiment.
content: Somehow, the project reached 100 Hosts. It was not elegant, but it worked. Experiment #001 asked people to interact publicly, drop EVM wallets, and become part of a strange distribution test with no forms, no whitelist, and no polished corporate process.

title: Token Finds Hosts
summary: A simple growth experiment was written down: if people do not find the token, the token finds people.
content: The earliest growth idea was blunt and strange. If people would not find the token, the token would find people. It became the seed for Experiment #001 and the later Host language.

title: Reason To Return
summary: The project shifted from chart attention toward daily visible building.
content: The founder wrote down the real problem: anyone can launch a memecoin, but very few build anything after that. The target changed. Visitors should not come back because of a chart. They should come back because something new exists every day.

title: Cold Pillow Host Signal
summary: A small purchase became the first funny Host signal in the archive.
content: Someone bought roughly 35 dollars of 0XB20. It was the largest observed purchase of that moment. The archive keeps it because small human signals matter more to this project than inflated launch theater.

title: Survived Another Day
summary: The founder woke up, the project still existed, and the first honest build log appeared.
content: The project survived another day. That alone was progress. Instead of chasing market noise, the founder started asking what the organism should become. A few ideas were stupid. One of them might work.

title: Laboratory Home Begins
summary: Work began on the official website because the 0XB20 universe needed a home.
content: By day two, the token had 50+ Hosts, the Genesis collection was live, and the community was active enough to need a permanent interface. The first website work began. The Laboratory needed a place to exist outside the feed.

title: Genesis Colony Catalogued
summary: The first 11 Genesis Specimens received names, numbers, and roles.
content: The Genesis Colony was catalogued: Spore, Seed, Fork, Relay, Cache, Hook, Gasling, Beacon, Immutable, Zero, and Keeper. The list turned the collection from images into a specimen database.

title: Funding Without Treasury
summary: The Genesis NFTs were framed as the project development engine.
content: 0XB20 had no VC, no team allocation, and no treasury. Genesis NFTs became the practical way to fund tools, content, experiments, and growth. Early collectors were recorded as people who found the parasites first.

title: Genesis Parasites Discovered
summary: The first NFT collection entered the 0XB20 universe as discovered digital organisms.
content: Something was living on Base. The Genesis Onchain Parasites were introduced as 11 Specimens born from blocks, gas, data, and unexplained corners of the network. They were not described as minted products. They were documented as discovered organisms.

title: Day Two Notebook
summary: The founder sketched possible next moves, then the project later refined them into stricter laboratory language.
content: The early notebook mentioned NFTs, airdrops, community roles, contributor rewards, and ecosystem outreach. Some of the wording was too launch-thread shaped. The useful signal remained: the founder was trying to turn a token into a living public experiment.

title: First Metrics Observed
summary: The first market and Host signals were recorded without keeping the hype language.
content: Early public posts tracked price movement, holder count, immutable contract status, and visible wallets. The archive removes the pressure language and keeps the part that matters: the organism had started producing measurable signals.

title: Day One Recap
summary: Day one closed with the token live, X verified, Telegram open, and 22 early Hosts.
content: The first recap recorded a live Base token, verified X account, open Telegram community, early roadmap thinking, and 22 Hosts. The post was noisy, but the project was already documenting its own survival.

title: Community Hub Opened
summary: The first Telegram hub went live and gave early Hosts a place to gather.
content: The community chat and update channel opened on day one. The original announcement used launch noise. The cleaned archive records the practical event: the project gained a public room.

title: Early Roadmap Draft Archived
summary: The first roadmap thread was preserved as evidence of the chaotic launch layer, not as a promise.
content: An early roadmap draft listed awakening, community, recognition, and an unrealistic final phase. The current Laboratory does not use that style. The archive keeps it as a fossil: proof that the project moved away from generic hype and toward honest build logs.

title: Native Standard Signal
summary: Base described the B20 Native Token Standard, and 0XB20 attached itself to that cultural signal.
content: The early account reacted to Base explaining native B20 tokens. The cleaned record is simple: Base had a native token standard, and 0XB20 became the unofficial organism growing around that standard.

title: First Awake Hosts
summary: The first morning posts framed 0XB20 as something early Hosts could discover before the feed noticed.
content: The account posted a morning signal about people still sleeping while the token was already live. The archive removes the pressure tactics and keeps the useful part: discovery was happening before the project had structure.

title: Origin Signal Verified
summary: The project began as a Base-native meme sparked by a public post and verified on X.
content: A post from Jesse Pollak sparked the idea. That idea became a token on Base, then the account received X verification. The first public identity was noisy, but the core signal was already there: built on Base, no presale, no VC layer, and a community experiment forming in public.
